setting.note: The Devil comes to atheist Soviet Moscow and turns it inside out. The map runs through the real city Bulgakov wrote from: a bench by Patriarch's Ponds, the writers' club drawn from Herzen House on Tverskoy Boulevard, and the haunted flat modelled on his own rooms on Bolshaya Sadovaya. A second thread, Pontius Pilate's Jerusalem, runs outside the city as a story within the story.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Mikhail Bulgakov, written 1928-1940 and first published 1966-1967. This book is mapped as a cited work: we store none of its text and reproduce no passages. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: The Master and Margarita, Mikhail Bulgakov (1967). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author or publisher.

palette.note: The deep red of the Devil's Moscow: the tram at Patriarch's Ponds and the fires that close the book. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
